name: Manzanares from Valdepeñas – circular via Membrilla and the río Azuer
name_original: Manzanares desde Valdepeñas – circular por Membrilla y el río Azuer
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Valdepeñas traverse a landscape characterized by expansive plains, agricultural areas, and river valleys, with varied elevation gains. The region offers a mix of rural roads connecting towns and villages, providing diverse cycling experiences. Terrain ranges from relatively flat sections along rivers to routes with significant climbs, catering to different fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Valdepeñas The most popular road cycling route is Manzanares from Valdepeñas – circular via Membrilla and the río Azuer, a 40.6 miles (65.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 34 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a circular journey through the local countryside and along the río Azuer. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Solana and Alcubillas from Val, a difficult 75.0 miles (120.8 km) path. This route features substantial elevation gain, passing through the towns of La Solana and Alcubillas. Local road cyclists also love the Don Quixote Statue – Plaza de España - Valdepeñas loop from Valdepeñas, a 27.8 miles (44.8 km) trail leading through urban and surrounding areas, often completed in about 1 hour 50 minutes. Road cycling around Valdepeñas is defined by rural roads, river valleys, and rolling hills. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, accommodating various ability levels. The routes in Valdepeñas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdepeñas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Valdepeñas?
A: There are over a dozen road cycling routes around Valdepeñas, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The region's landscape features expansive plains, agricultural areas, and river valleys.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Valdepeñas?
A: Yes, Valdepeñas offers several easy road cycling routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129911" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Don Quixote Statue – Plaza de España - Valdepeñas loop from Valdepeñas&lt;/a&gt;, which is 27.8 miles (44.8 km) long and takes about 1 hour 50 minutes to complete, leading through urban and surrounding areas.

Q: What are some options for longer road cycling tours around Valdepeñas?
A: For longer rides, consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085935203/la-solana-and-alcubillas-from-val" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Solana and Alcubillas from Val&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 75.0 miles (120.8 km) path with substantial elevation gain. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40016465" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bar Los Andaluces – Valdepeñas loop from Valdepeñas&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 97.5 km.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs in Valdepeñas?
A: Yes, for cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085935203/la-solana-and-alcubillas-from-val" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Solana and Alcubillas from Val&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain, testing your endurance across the varied terrain of the region.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Valdepeñas?
A: The best time for road cycling in Valdepeñas is typically during the spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler temperatures of winter.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Valdepeñas?
A: Many routes in Valdepeñas are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1080470201/manzanares-from-valdepenas-circular-via-membrilla-and-the-rio-azuer" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares from Valdepeñas – circular via Membrilla and the río Azuer&lt;/a&gt;, a 40.6-mile (65.4 km) route.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Valdepeñas pass through or near towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel and rest. The region's rural roads often connect these local communities.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints or interesting sights along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes often traverse agricultural landscapes and river valleys, offering picturesque views. You can also pass through historic towns. Notable landmarks include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1400095" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;town of Valdepeñas&lt;/a&gt; itself, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3984586" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza España&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3451633" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arroyo del Peral&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Valdepeñas?
A: The road cycling experience in Valdepeñas is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, which includes rural roads, river valleys, and rolling hills, accommodating various ability levels.

Q: Is parking readily available near the road cycling routes in Valdepeñas?
A: Valdepeñas, as a regional hub, generally offers parking options within the town and near common starting points for routes. Many routes begin directly from Valdepeñas, making it convenient to park and start your ride.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the local towns and villages?
A: Yes, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40008528" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Membrilla – Plaza de España loop from El Alamillo&lt;/a&gt; take you through local towns and villages, allowing you to experience the regional culture and architecture while cycling.
tips: In it is the town hall as well as the Parroquia de la Asunción de Nuestra Señora Church.

The Manzanares Castle (Castillo de Pilas Bonas) is included in the Inventory of Architectural Heritage of Historical-Artistic Interest carried out by the Ministry of Culture in 1979. Although there are still many dark points regarding the origin of the Castle and the town of Manzanares, various historians agree that the Castle must have been built around the year 1239, after the demarcation of the territories of the orders of Santiago and Calatrava.
The reasons for its construction would probably be the proximity to the Azuer River, the existence of wells with abundant water or the crossing of roads and trails.

The origins of the town of Membrilla can be traced back to an ancient Greek colony settled in this place known as Marmaria. Marmaria was already inhabited a long time before, all sources point to the mound that housed the Castillo del Tocón, today known as the Virgen del Espino Hermitage. Over time it would become known as Marmellaria, in the Middle Ages it would be Membriella, from where it would become La Membrilla.
Two enormous traditional stoneware storage containers mark the old southern entrance to a historic farm that's now closer to the highway with a more direct access a few hundred meters north.

The Baños del Peral are located in Valdepeñas, in the province of Ciudad Real. Originally, this place was a bathhouse that operated from the first third of the 19th century until the beginning of the 20th century. The waters of the spring of the Baños del Peral are known for their mineral-medicinal properties. 
Currently, the building has been restored and converted into a Water Interpretation Centre, where you can learn about the importance of baths in the region and the properties of water. The centre has exhibition rooms that recall how people used to go to this place to seek comfort and solutions to muscular or mental ailments.

In it is the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption, occupies one of the flanks of the Plaza Mayor of the town. In plan it has two Herrerian-influenced naves: the main one and San Lorenzo, which is shorter, and a Mannerist chapel on the gospel side.
In the main nave is the altarpiece, a copy of the one from 1553, destroyed in the Civil War, of which six panels have been preserved that correspond to a pre-Mannerist style prior to Correa de Vivar, which some hypotheses attribute to the Hernando Yánez School ( disciple of Raphael). At the end of this nave, the choir on a pointed arch.
In the nave of San Lorenzo, a work by Gregorio Prieto with the figure of the patron saint, the Virgin of Consolation.
